notes.note: Cicero is pressing to recover the dowry he had given for Tullia's marriage to Dolabella, repayable in installments after their divorce; the matter is bound up with Balbus's offer to take over ("assign") the debt. Settling it would free funds for the projected shrine to Tullia.

notes.anchor: Balbus's terms for assigning the debt are princely
notes.note: L. Cornelius Balbus, Caesar's chief financial agent, has made generous terms for the legal transfer ("delegatio") of a debt owed to Cicero, redirecting it to a new payer. "Princely" (regia) marks how lavish the offer is.

Renders the Greek "apotheōsin": Cicero's plan to build a shrine (fanum) consecrating his dead daughter Tullia as a kind of deity. He weighs his island at Arpinum as a site but fears its remoteness would diminish the honour, and turns instead to suburban gardens near Rome.

The "horti" are suburban pleasure-gardens across the Tiber from Rome, which Cicero hunts through this book as a public, visited site for Tullia's shrine. Their accessibility is exactly what the remote island at Arpinum lacks.

Cicero and Atticus are discussing whether to retain an Epicurean speaker among the cast of characters in one of Cicero's philosophical dialogues; "as you wish" yields the point, though Cicero adds he will "make the adjustment." Atticus was himself an Epicurean.
